title: RLHF vs. DPO vs. Online DPO/RLOO
subtitle: Three ways to teach a model what you prefer — same objective, different machinery.

methodCards.name: RLHF
methodCards.tagline: Train a reward model, then run PPO against it
methodCards.formula: Policy + Reference + Reward model + Value network
bullets: Reward model scores responses from human preference pairs
PPO nudges the policy toward higher-scoring responses
KL penalty (β) anchors the policy to a frozen reference

methodCards.name: DPO
methodCards.tagline: Skip the reward model — a closed-form loss on pairs
bullets: Only 2 models in memory: policy and reference
The default for most teams, most of the time

methodCards.name: Online DPO / RLOO
methodCards.tagline: Judge the current policy's own fresh samples
methodCards.formula: Sample from policy → judge ranks on the spot
bullets: Fresh signal, graded on live samples, not stale pairs
Needs a judge (model or rule-based) every round

rlhf.value: Sensitive to β; prone to collapse

dpo.value: Static — goes stale as policy improves

whenToChoose.heading: Choose DPO when
bullets: You want a stable, predictable, supervised-style loss
You don't need a separate reward model

whenToChoose.heading: Choose Online DPO / RLOO when
bullets: The policy has moved past what your offline pairs describe
You have a judge (model or rule-based) available each round

workedExample.heading: The DPO loss, worked through
workedExample.body: One pair partway through training: the chosen response's log-probability rose from −12.4 to −9.8 (+2.6 nats); the rejected response's fell from −11.0 to −13.5 (−2.5 nats). Scaled by β=0.1: 0.1 × [2.6 − (−2.5)] = 0.51. Through the sigmoid, −log σ(0.51) ≈ 0.47 — down from −log σ(0) = ln(2) ≈ 0.69 at the very start of training. That drop is DPO doing its job across a few epochs of 8,000 pairs.

coreIntuition.heading: Same objective, fewer moving parts
coreIntuition.body: RLHF and DPO optimize the same objective — preferred responses more likely, rejected ones less likely, without drifting too far from the reference policy. DPO just derives a closed-form loss for that objective instead of fitting a reward model and running PPO against it: fewer moving parts, same target.

interviewQuestions.question: Why does the KL penalty matter, and what happens if you remove it entirely from either method?
When would you still reach for PPO-style RLHF over DPO, even knowing it costs more?
interviewQuestions.hint: Fine-grained reward shaping, existing RL infra.

interviewQuestions.question: How would you detect reward hacking in an RLHF pipeline before it ships?

productionConsiderations.text: Tune β whenever the model, data, or task changes — it's the knob between learning the preference and collapsing toward the reference.
Static preference data goes stale once the policy improves past whatever generated the pairs.
A model that got safer can have quietly lost ground on reasoning or coding — measure the alignment tax, don't assume it away.
Human preference isn't ground truth — where correctness is mechanically checkable, a verifier beats any judge.

checklist.text: DPO not dismissed as an inferior shortcut without checking your own evals
β revisited whenever the model, data, or task changes
Preference data refreshed instead of training offline forever on one static batch
Capability regression measured alongside alignment metrics
Correctness verified mechanically wherever the task allows it

keyTakeaways: RLHF and DPO optimize the same objective; DPO reaches it with a closed-form loss instead of a reward model and PPO
DPO needs half the models in memory (2 vs. 4) and trains far more predictably
The frozen reference policy is the guardrail in both methods
Static preference data has a shelf life; online variants trade staleness for judge infrastructure

nodes.label: Does the task have a mechanically checkable answer?
Has the policy moved past your preference data?
Online DPO / RLOO, or RLHF
nodes.sublabel: Fresh judged samples, or full reward-model infra
